text: Slovakia’s politics has undergone a profound change as new anti-corruption, anti-elitist and populist parties emerged to power under the banner of combating corruption, the political elite, as well as by promising a change in politics, or by populist or even Eurosceptic rhetoric, hence breaking the pattern of alternating right and left coalition formations. While internally very diverse, the new political actors were able to establish effective governments and even formally agree to coalitions. Thus, Slovak coalition governance continued to exhibit a high degree of stability at the ministerial level and along the lines of the model of a ‘ministerial government’, based on a detailed, publicly available contract defining the distribution of ministers’ portfolios, rules of interaction among coalition members, and an associated ‘coalition council’ tasked with resolving potential disputes. This pattern proved to be robust even in the face of the challenges presented by the financial crisis and the migration crisis, which – as in the other CEE countries – had no considerable negative impact on democratic quality. It was only the COVID-19 crisis that provided an opportunity for Prime Ministers to strengthen their position in relation to the other coalition parties and to a degree to undermine the achievements of the recent development of more coalition-friendly governance in Slovakia.
